critic_raw:
The methodology is generally well-constructed — temporal splits are used correctly, preprocessing artifacts are fit on training only, the baseline is tuned with comparable rigor, and the authors are explicit about what the experiment can and cannot establish. That said, there are a few substantive issues worth examining.

**Issue 1: The 'first-assignment interval' feature is the most likely source of label leakage and is not sufficiently scrutinized.**

The design includes 'elapsed minutes from ticket creation to first assignment into an agent queue' as a metadata feature, described as reflecting 'operational load at intake.' The claim is that this is a property available at prediction time. But consider the mechanics: if the model is meant to predict SLA breach *before* breach occurs (i.e., to triage or intervene early), the prediction must be made at some specific point in the ticket lifecycle. The first-assignment interval is not available until assignment happens. If prediction is made at creation time, this feature does not exist. If prediction is made at assignment time, that may be fine — but the design never specifies the prediction horizon. This ambiguity matters because the first-assignment interval is almost certainly correlated with eventual SLA breach (a ticket that sits unassigned for 40 minutes is already behind schedule), which means if the feature is legitimately available at prediction time it is doing a lot of work, and if it is not legitimately available, the multi-task model has an artificial advantage. The baseline (text-only logistic regression on TF-IDF) cannot use this feature at all, so the comparison does not isolate 'multi-task learning' — it also conflates 'having operational load signals.' Evidence one way or the other: specify the exact wall-clock point at which predictions are generated in production, verify this is applied consistently across both model and baseline, and run an ablation removing first-assignment interval from the multi-task model to decompose its contribution.

**Issue 2: Auxiliary task labels are almost certainly constructed from the same or overlapping information as the target label, creating a subtle but real leakage path.**

The multi-task model jointly predicts ticket category, urgency level, and SLA breach. The design does not explain how category and urgency labels are obtained. In practice, these are almost always annotated by support agents *after* processing the ticket — which means they may incorporate knowledge of the outcome (whether an SLA was actually breached, how difficult the ticket turned out to be). If urgency labels are assigned retrospectively by agents who know the ticket's resolution trajectory, then training the shared backbone to predict urgency is equivalent to supervised pre-training on a noisy proxy of breach outcome. This inflates the multi-task model's performance relative to a baseline that has no access to this retrospectively-constructed signal. The specific claim being made is that auxiliary tasks provide independent supervisory signal that helps the model generalize — but if those labels are derived from the same event window as the breach label, this assumption breaks down. Evidence: audit the timestamp of urgency/category label assignment relative to ticket close time and SLA breach determination. If the two are correlated in timing, the auxiliary task benefit is not clean. A direct test would be an ablation where auxiliary task heads are removed and only breach is predicted with the full metadata feature set.

**Issue 3: The confound control via SHAP is methodologically insufficient to support the claim it is meant to support.**

The design says: 'If the multi-task model improves primarily by learning customer tier or product area, the gain is not meaningful. We compute feature attribution (SHAP, attention weights) to verify.' This misunderstands what SHAP can establish. SHAP measures marginal contribution of features to predictions, not what the model has learned in its shared representation. A model can use customer tier and product area heavily in its latent space while appearing to have low SHAP attribution for those features if they are correlated with other inputs (e.g., text content for enterprise vs. SMB customers is stylistically different). SHAP will in that case attribute the variance to text features even though tier is the true driver. The claim — that SHAP can verify the improvement is not driven by easy predictors — is not supportable at the mechanism level. The per-subgroup recall at 5% FPR is a more direct test. Evidence that this matters: if tier and product area are strong marginal predictors of breach (which they almost certainly are, given that SLA thresholds are defined per-tier), SHAP on the multi-task model will understate their role when they are encoded in the text distribution. The correct control is to train a separate model on metadata features only (no text) and measure how much of the multi-task model's gain is explained by that simpler model.

**Issue 4: Threshold calibration on the validation set introduces a subtle leakage into the primary metric.**

The primary metric is Recall at 5% FPR on the test set, where the threshold is 'set on the validation set to achieve ≤ 5% FPR, then applied to the test set.' This is correct in principle, but the validation FPR will not exactly equal the test FPR due to distributional shift across time periods (which the design itself acknowledges as a concern via temporal drift checks). If the threshold is tuned to ≤ 5% FPR on months 25–30 and the FPR on months 31–36 is actually 7–8%, the reported recall at '5% FPR' is not meaningful — the model is operating at a different operating point than claimed. This is not a leakage problem per se but a measurement validity problem. The comparison between models could also be distorted asymmetrically: if the multi-task model's score distribution is more stable across the validation-to-test shift than the baseline's, it will appear to have better recall at 5% FPR in part because its threshold generalizes better, not because it detects breaches more effectively. Evidence: report the actual empirical FPR of each model on the test set when using the validation-calibrated threshold, and compute recall at the *actual* achieved FPR rather than the nominal 5% target.

**Summary:** Issues 1 and 2 are the most serious — they concern whether the multi-task model is genuinely learning from multi-task structure or from features and labels that are retrospectively contaminated or unavailable at the intended prediction horizon. Issue 3 is a methodological gap in the proposed confound control. Issue 4 is a metric validity concern under distribution shift.
defender_raw:
## Pass 1 — Analysis of Critic's Four Issues

### Issue 1: First-Assignment Interval and Prediction Horizon Ambiguity

**Critic's claim:** The 'elapsed minutes from ticket creation to first assignment' feature may not be available at prediction time (if predictions are made at ticket creation), giving the multi-task model an unfair advantage over the text-only baseline and potentially constituting temporal leakage. The comparison conflates 'multi-task learning' with 'having operational load signals.'

**Assessment:** This is a valid and serious critique. The design document does not specify the exact wall-clock point at which predictions are generated in production. This is a genuine omission. The critic is correct that:

1. First-assignment interval is definitionally unavailable at ticket creation time.
2. First-assignment interval is almost certainly strongly predictive of SLA breach — a ticket sitting unassigned for 40 minutes is already consuming its SLA budget.
3. The text-only baseline has no access to this feature, so the comparison does not purely isolate multi-task learning value.
4. Without a stated prediction horizon, we cannot verify the feature is legitimately available.

**Counter-argument (partial):** The feature is described as 'available in the support system' and derived from 'early workflow signals.' It is plausible that the intended prediction horizon is post-assignment (i.e., the model is meant to flag tickets at the moment of first queue assignment, not at creation). Many operational triage systems work exactly this way — a ticket is routed, and then an ML system flags whether it is at risk. In that deployment model, the feature is clean. However, the design never makes this explicit, and the ablation the critic requests (removing first-assignment interval and measuring its isolated contribution) was not planned.

**Concession:** The critic is right that we need to (a) state the prediction horizon explicitly, (b) verify the baseline operates at the same temporal cut, and (c) run an ablation isolating first-assignment interval's contribution. The current design has a real gap here. This is not merely a documentation issue — if first-assignment interval is doing most of the work, the claimed benefit of multi-task learning is overstated. I concede this as a critical issue requiring remediation.

**What remains uncertain:** Whether the feature is legitimate depends on the deployment specification, which was not written into the design. The fix is straightforward but the current design is incomplete.

---

### Issue 2: Auxiliary Task Label Construction and Retrospective Contamination

**Critic's claim:** Ticket category and urgency labels are almost certainly annotated retrospectively by agents who know the ticket's outcome, making supervised auxiliary task training equivalent to pre-training on a noisy proxy of the breach outcome. This inflates multi-task model performance.

**Assessment:** This is also a valid and serious critique. The design is completely silent on the provenance of auxiliary task labels (category and urgency). In the overwhelming majority of support platforms, these labels are assigned during or after ticket resolution, not at creation or triage time. If that is true here:

1. Urgency labels assigned by agents who have seen the full ticket trajectory encode retrospective knowledge of breach risk.
2. Training the shared backbone to predict urgency amounts to training it to approximately predict breach from a signal that encodes breach-adjacent information.
3. The multi-task model would then appear to be better at 'generalizing from text and metadata' when it is actually benefiting from a disguised form of breach-correlated supervision.

**Counter-argument (partial):** There is a scenario in which this critique does not apply — if urgency and category are assigned during intake triage, before ticket resolution, as part of a routing protocol. Some support systems do this. If intake agents assign urgency at the moment of first contact based only on the ticket content and the customer's tier, without knowledge of SLA breach outcome, then the labels are clean. Additionally, even retrospectively-assigned urgency labels may provide legitimate generalization signal if urgency is a stable semantic property of the ticket text that precedes breach causally.

**Concession:** However, the design provides no evidence that labels are assigned prospectively, and the critic's default assumption (retrospective annotation) is the more common case. The required audit — checking the timestamp of urgency/category label assignment relative to ticket close and breach determination — was not planned. This is a critical gap. A direct ablation (multi-task model with only breach head, full metadata) would decompose the multi-task contribution cleanly. I concede this as a critical issue. Without the label provenance audit, the core experimental claim (that multi-task learning adds value) cannot be verified.

**What remains uncertain:** Whether labels are prospective or retrospective is an empirical question about the specific support platform. The fix is auditable, but the current design did not plan for it.

---

### Issue 3: SHAP Attribution Insufficiency for Confound Control

**Critic's claim:** SHAP measures marginal feature contribution, not latent representation content. If tier and product area are correlated with text style (e.g., enterprise vs. SMB language patterns), SHAP will attribute variance to text even when tier is the true driver. A metadata-only baseline ablation is the correct control.

**Assessment:** This is a methodologically correct critique. The critic accurately describes a well-known limitation of SHAP in the presence of correlated features. Specifically:

1. SHAP's Shapley values distribute credit across correlated features in ways that do not reflect causal pathways.
2. If enterprise customers write tickets in systematically different language than SMB customers (very plausible — different vocabulary, formality, issue types), the transformer embedding will encode tier implicitly.
3. SHAP on text embeddings will attribute variance to text features, making it appear that the model learned meaningful semantic content, when it may have learned a proxy for tier.
4. The design's claim that 'SHAP can verify improvement is not driven by easy predictors' overstates what SHAP can establish.

**Counter-argument:** The per-subgroup recall at 5% FPR stratified by tier and product area is a more direct test and was planned. This is partially responsive to the concern — if the model performs similarly across tiers, it is less likely that tier alone is driving results. However, the critic is right that a metadata-only model (tier + product area + other structured features, no text) would directly quantify how much of the multi-task gain is explained by easy tabular predictors. This ablation is not in the design.

**Concession:** I concede that SHAP is insufficient as the primary mechanism check and that the design overstates its diagnostic value. The per-subgroup analysis is useful but not a substitute for a metadata-only ablation baseline. Adding this ablation is straightforward and the design should have included it. This is a real methodological gap, though it is less critical than Issues 1 and 2 — it affects the interpretability of positive results rather than the validity of the primary comparison.

**What remains uncertain:** Whether tier/area are encoded implicitly in embeddings is an empirical question, but it is sufficiently likely in support ticket data that the ablation is necessary.

---

### Issue 4: Threshold Calibration Validity Under Temporal Drift

**Critic's claim:** The validation-calibrated threshold targeting ≤5% FPR may not achieve exactly 5% FPR on the test set due to distributional shift, making the reported 'Recall at 5% FPR' measurement invalid. Asymmetric threshold generalization between models could distort the comparison.

**Assessment:** This is a valid measurement validity concern, though it is less severe than Issues 1 and 2. The critic correctly identifies that:

1. Temporal distribution shift (explicitly acknowledged by the design) means score distributions in months 31–36 may differ from months 25–30.
2. If the actual test FPR is 7–8% when the nominal target was 5%, the reported recall is not at the claimed operating point.
3. If multi-task and baseline models have different degrees of score distribution stability across the temporal gap, the comparison at a nominal 5% FPR threshold could favor whichever model's scores happen to generalize better, for reasons unrelated to SLA detection quality.

**Counter-argument:** The critic's proposed fix — report actual empirical FPR on the test set and compute recall at the actual achieved FPR — is straightforward and does not require redesigning the experiment. It is also standard practice and should have been included. However, the severity of this issue depends on how large the temporal shift is. The design already reports performance on months 34–36 separately, which provides some evidence of drift. Reporting the empirical operating point on the test set is an easy addition.

**Concession:** The design is incomplete here. The primary metric definition should include reporting the actual achieved FPR on the test set alongside the recall figure. The critic is right that comparing models at a nominal rather than actual FPR is a measurement validity problem. I concede this as a real but remediable gap — less critical than Issues 1 and 2, but real.

**What remains uncertain:** The magnitude of the threshold generalization problem depends on the degree of temporal drift, which is empirically determinable.

---

## Pass 2 — Verdict Selection

Summarizing Pass 1 concessions:

- **Issue 1:** Conceded as critical. Prediction horizon is unspecified; first-assignment interval may be unavailable at prediction time; baseline comparison is confounded; ablation was not planned.
- **Issue 2:** Conceded as critical. Auxiliary task label provenance is unaudited; retrospective annotation is the more common case; this creates a plausible leakage path through the shared backbone that would inflate multi-task model performance.
- **Issue 3:** Conceded as real methodological gap. SHAP overstated as diagnostic tool; metadata-only ablation baseline is missing.
- **Issue 4:** Conceded as real measurement validity gap. Empirical FPR should be reported on the test set.

Issues 1 and 2 are independently capable of invalidating the core experimental claim. Issue 1 means the comparison may not isolate multi-task learning. Issue 2 means the auxiliary task benefit may be a leakage artifact rather than a genuine generalization gain. Together, they are not addressable by argument — they require empirical verification (auditing label timestamps, running ablations, specifying and enforcing the prediction horizon). Issues 3 and 4 are remediable through straightforward additions to the experimental protocol.

Given two conceded critical flaws that cannot be resolved without empirical investigation, the verdict must be `empirical_test_agreed`. The methodology is not fundamentally misconceived — the temporal split, preprocessing discipline, and metric alignment are all sound — but the core comparison is not clean as designed, and whether it can be made clean depends on facts about the support platform (label annotation timing, deployment prediction horizon) that must be empirically verified.
all_issues_raised: The 'first-assignment interval' feature may not be available at the intended prediction time, giving the multi-task model an unfair advantage over the text-only baseline and potentially constituting temporal leakage.

Auxiliary task labels (ticket category, urgency) are likely assigned retrospectively by agents with knowledge of ticket outcomes, creating a leakage path through the shared backbone that inflates multi-task model performance.

SHAP-based attribution is insufficient to verify that gains are not driven by easy predictors (tier, product area) when those predictors are correlated with the text distribution; a metadata-only baseline ablation is the correct control.

Threshold calibration at 5% FPR on the validation set may not transfer to the test set under temporal drift, making the primary metric measurement invalid and potentially distorting model comparisons asymmetrically.
all_issues_adjudicated: The 'first-assignment interval' feature lacks a specified prediction horizon, creating a real risk of temporal leakage and conflating operational load signals with multi-task learning benefits — the defender conceded this as critical and requiring ablation.

Auxiliary task labels (category, urgency) are likely assigned retrospectively with knowledge of ticket outcomes, creating a plausible leakage path through the shared backbone that could inflate multi-task model performance — the defender conceded this as critical and requiring a label provenance audit.

SHAP attribution is insufficient to verify that gains are not driven by easy predictors correlated with text distributions; a metadata-only baseline ablation is necessary and missing — the defender conceded this as a real methodological gap.

Threshold calibration at a nominal 5% FPR on the validation set may not transfer to the test set under temporal drift, making the primary metric measurement potentially invalid and the model comparison asymmetrically distorted — the defender conceded this as a real measurement validity gap.
